candidate_problems.text: Can the proposed 'equilibrium lock-in' be made into a precise theorem rather than an analogy? The core open problem is to formalize the trade-off surface among internality, soundness, completeness, decidability/tractability, and action-guidance, and then prove a no-local-improvement or Pareto-frontier result for self-modeling agents. Without this, it remains unclear whether the Nash-like language is explanatory or merely metaphorical.

What exact class of agents and frameworks does the barrier apply to, and where are the boundary cases? The conjecture gestures at 'sufficiently expressive agents,' but it is unresolved how the result changes for bounded reasoners, probabilistic reasoners, reflective oracles, typed/hierarchical languages, resource-limited agents, or systems that relax full soundness. Exploring these cases could reveal whether the lock-in is universal, graded, or avoidable under practically important architectures.

How should 'sound, action-relevant self-knowledge' be defined so that the notion is both nontrivial and operational? The conjecture depends on action-relevance and soundness under self-execution, but these are underspecified: does action-relevance mean predictive control, policy improvement, calibration, or robustness under reflection? Clarifying this may change the whole picture, because the impossibility might attach only to one demanding notion of self-knowledge while weaker but useful forms remain achievable.
